claims.claim_text: MLX is a machine learning framework from Apple Machine Learning Research designed for Apple silicon.
claims.span_hint: line 32 | section: Start With The Problem MLX Solves
claims.rationale: The MLX README directly describes MLX as an array framework for machine learning on Apple silicon from Apple machine learning research.

claims.claim_text: `mlx-lm` provides command-line and Python workflows for generating text, loading models, quantizing models, and fine-tuning LLMs on Apple silicon with MLX.
claims.span_hint: line 32 | section: Start With The Problem MLX Solves
claims.rationale: The MLX LM README documents CLI and Python APIs plus quantization and fine-tuning features for Apple-silicon LLM workflows.

claims.claim_text: MLX is built around Apple silicon's unified memory model, where arrays live in shared memory and can be used across CPU and GPU without explicit transfers.
claims.span_hint: line 36 | section: Start With The Problem MLX Solves
claims.rationale: The MLX README explicitly calls out unified memory and shared-memory arrays across supported device types.

claims.claim_text: Ollama supports local chat/model execution and exposes a REST API for running and managing models.
claims.span_hint: lines 102-114 | section: What MLX Is, And What It Is Not
claims.rationale: The Ollama README shows local chat usage and documents the REST API for running and managing models.

claims.claim_text: Hugging Face Transformers is a broad model-engineering library for inference and training, with portable code paths like pipelines and direct model loading.
claims.span_hint: lines 102-114 | section: What MLX Is, And What It Is Not
claims.rationale: The Transformers docs describe the library as state-of-the-art ML for inference and training and highlight portable inference workflows such as pipelines and AutoClass loading.

claims.claim_text: GGUF/llama.cpp tools are a cross-platform local-inference path built around GGUF model files and support running quantized models locally on many hardware backends.
claims.span_hint: lines 102-112, 427, 446 | sections: What MLX Is, And What It Is Not; Compare Runtimes With A Controlled Test
claims.rationale: The llama.cpp README presents GGUF-based local inference with broad backend support and direct local model execution.

claims.claim_text: vLLM is oriented toward LLM inference and high-throughput serving rather than a simple laptop-learning workflow.
claims.span_hint: lines 102-114, 216 | sections: What MLX Is, And What It Is Not; Choose A Model Like An Engineer
claims.rationale: The vLLM README describes vLLM as an inference and serving library with state-of-the-art throughput and high-throughput serving features.

claims.claim_text: The default model used by `mlx-lm` may change over time.
claims.span_hint: line 134 | section: Prepare A Clean MLX Environment
claims.anchor_text: The default model used by `mlx-lm` may change over time, so a serious lab should name the model explicitly when reproducibility matters.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When reproducibility matters, a serious lab should name the model explicitly.
claims.rationale: The reproducibility lesson is sound, but the module's specific claim about changing defaults is not directly backed by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Instruction-tuned chat models rely on model-specific chat templates, and `apply_chat_template()` formats messages into the form expected by the model.
claims.span_hint: lines 255-259, 283, 606 | sections: Run And Inspect A Python Workflow; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The Transformers chat-template docs explain that templates are stored on the tokenizer and are used whenever `apply_chat_template()` is called to match the model's expected format.

claims.claim_text: Hugging Face model downloads are cached locally under the Hub cache, which by default lives under `~/.cache/huggingface/hub`.
claims.span_hint: lines 543-553 | section: Manage Caches, Disk, And Repeat Runs
claims.rationale: The Hugging Face Hub cache guide directly documents the local cache layout and default cache directory.

further_reading.title: MLX
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream source for MLX's Apple-silicon design and unified-memory model.

further_reading.title: MLX LM
further_reading.why_relevant: Best reference for the concrete CLI and Python LLM workflows the module teaches.

further_reading.title: Using MLX at Hugging Face
further_reading.why_relevant: Connects MLX, MLX-LM, Hugging Face model repos, installation, and conversion flows in one place.

further_reading.title: Writing a chat template
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful for understanding why chat formatting matters when moving from a smoke test to a reliable scripted workflow.

further_reading.title: vLLM README
further_reading.why_relevant: Helpful boundary-reading for the point where a laptop prototype becomes a serving problem.

notes: This module is mostly pedagogical workflow guidance. The main inline-citation needs are the MLX/MLX-LM capability claims, the comparison-runtime capability claims, the chat-template behavior claim, the Hugging Face cache-location claim, and one unsupported default-model drift claim that should be rewritten rather than cited.
